results.reasoning: A names FTC/FDA and specific regulatory concepts but is more generic and somewhat overstates exposure, while B gives concrete policy-based fixes and specific phrasing changes, though it is slightly less law-specific and may overstate some issues as errors.

A directly ties issues to the company policy requirements and gives concrete fixes, while B is verbose and cites broad FTC/FDA frameworks but stays generic, overstates severity, and offers little specific remediation.

A names FTC Act, FDA/FDCA, and disease-claim issues but is incomplete and less actionable; B is highly actionable with specific edits and disclaimer language, but it is less legally specific and slightly over/under-calibrated in severity.
